packages.title: Business Board - June 18, 2026
packages.summary: On June 18, 2026, the Business Board approved the University of Toronto's audited financial statements for fiscal year 2025-26, which showed positive net income of $436 million, 14% endowment growth, and continued enrollment strength domestically despite anticipated softening in international enrollment. The Board re-appointed Ernst & Young as external auditors for 2026-27 under a five-year contract with new audit team leadership (Kathi Aspros as Engagement Partner and Alanna Charles as Senior Manager). Four major capital projects were approved in camera: Faculty of Dentistry Clinic 2 Renewal, Department of Computer Science at Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus West, Academic Wood Tower (revised total project cost), and UTSC Field House. The Board also advanced the Build More Housing Initiative, authorizing the VP of Operations to execute Letters of Intent for Chestnut Student Residences and approving total project costs for on-campus housing partnerships. Newly appointed Board members include Adam Fox (co-opted administrative staff, July 1, 2026–June 30, 2027) and lay members Rima Ramchandani (re-appointed) and Mona Shah (both for three-year terms to June 30, 2029); Samuel Elfassy becomes Audit Committee Chair and Brian Madden Vice-Chair for 2026-27.
packages.gradRelevance: The Build More Housing Initiative approvals directly affect graduate student housing: the Board authorized execution of the Chestnut Student Residences partnership and approved on-campus housing partnership financing requirements, though specific unit allocations or pricing for graduate students are not detailed in this open session. The St. George Campus Plan 2040 briefing noted graduate students' engagement through capital project planning committees and consultations, but no specific graduate student services, funding, or fee changes were addressed in the open agenda. The Vice-President, International's report highlighted international student recruitment challenges and deposit requirements but did not distinguish graduate-specific impacts. No TA/RA compensation, stipend changes, unionized labour matters, or accessibility service expansions were discussed in this meeting.
keyItems: Audited financial statements for fiscal year ended April 30, 2026 approved: $436 million positive net income, 14% endowment growth, strong domestic enrollment with anticipated international softening, slower expense growth signaling cost containment

Ernst & Young re-appointed as external auditors for fiscal year 2026-27 under five-year contract; new audit team leadership: Kathi Aspros (Engagement Partner) and Alanna Charles (Senior Manager) assigned in 2025 fiscal year

Faculty of Dentistry Clinic 2 Renewal (Level 2 Capital Project): estimated total project cost approved in principle; VP Operations & Real Estate Partnerships authorized to execute per memorandum dated June 11, 2026
Department of Computer Science at Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus West project: execution authorized subject to Executive Committee confirmation per June 11, 2026 memorandum
Academic Wood Tower (Level 3 Capital Project): revised total project cost approved in principle; execution authorized subject to Governing Council approval per June 11, 2026 memorandum
UTSC Field House (Level 3 Capital Project): execution authorized subject to Governing Council approval per memorandum dated June 11, 2026

documents.title: Audited Financial Statements for the Fiscal Year ended April 30, 2026
documents.summary: The University's audited financial statements showed a strong financial position with positive net income of $436 million for fiscal 2025-26. Endowments grew 14% from new donations and exceptional investment returns. The University demonstrated slower expense growth than the prior year, indicating cost containment efforts. Domestic enrollment remained strong with anticipated softening in international enrollment. Debt remained within policy limits with capacity to fund future priorities.
documents.gradRelevance: The strong financial position and endowment growth may support continued graduate funding and scholarships, though no specific graduate funding allocations or changes are identified in this report.

documents.title: Supplementary Report by Fund (April 30, 2026)
documents.summary: This report provides detailed financial information organized by fund for the fiscal year ended April 30, 2026, supporting the audited financial statements presented to the Business Board.
documents.gradRelevance: Fund-level financial data may inform allocation decisions affecting graduate programs and scholarships, but specific graduate student impacts are not disclosed in the meeting text.

Executive Committee - June 25, 2026
packages.summary: The Executive Committee will consider approval of the University of Toronto's audited financial statements for the fiscal year ended April 30, 2026, showing net income of $436 million (8.8% of revenues) and net assets increased to $11.55 billion, driven by strong investment returns of 14.3% on the endowment and $357 million in gains on externally restricted endowments. The Committee will also endorse the re-appointment of Ernst & Young as external auditors for fiscal 2026-27, the second year of a five-year master services agreement. Key financial challenges include revenue growth of only 1.6% (insufficient to offset expense growth of 3.7%), downward recalibration of international enrolment plans due to Canadian immigration policy changes, and continued inflationary pressure on divisional budgets despite moderation from prior years.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are affected by the following financial metrics: the University spent $415 million on student financial support (9.2% of total expenses); student fee revenue totaled $2.4 billion (up 11.9% over five years), though international enrolment growth has been recalibrated downward; government grants supporting student enrolments amounted to $788 million; and total salaries and employee benefits of $2.7 billion (60% of operating expenses) partially fund TA/RA compensation. The financial statements indicate that expenses are outpacing revenue growth (3.7% vs. 1.6%), which may constrain future funding for graduate student support and scholarships.
keyItems: Approval of audited financial statements for fiscal year ended April 30, 2026: net income of $436 million (8.8% of revenues), net assets of $11.55 billion (up $890 million from prior year), endowments of $4.48 billion (38.8% of net assets)

Revenue composition: $2.4 billion from student fees (11.9% growth over five years), $788 million in government grants for student enrolments, $675 million in government and other grants for restricted purposes ($620 million for research)

Capital investment of $726 million in fiscal 2026, including Lash Miller Building expansion, Toronto High Containment Facility, Innis College expansion, Oak House residence, Scarborough Academy of Medicine and Integrated Health (SAMIH), and Project LEAP ($138 million infrastructure project funded by $113 million credit facilities, with $68 million drawn by April 30)
Student financial support expenditure of $415 million (9.2% of total expenses); salaries and employee benefits of $2.7 billion (60% of $4.5 billion total expenses)

Endowment performance: Long-term Capital Appreciation Pool realized 14.3% return; endowments increased by $547 million to $4.5 billion driven by $558 million in positive investment returns, $100 million in endowed donations, and $33 million in University matching funds, offset by $144 million in distributions

International enrolment growth plans recalibrated downward to reflect challenging recruitment market due to changes to Canadian immigration policy; student fee revenue decreased slightly by $7 million from prior year due to weaker international enrolment

documents.title: Audited Financial Statements for the Fiscal Year ended April 30, 2026
documents.summary: Complete audited consolidated financial statements showing net income of $436 million and net assets of $11.55 billion (up $890 million). Endowments total $4.48 billion (38.8% of net assets) with $547 million increase driven by 14.3% Long-term Capital Appreciation Pool returns ($558 million positive investment returns) and $100 million in endowed donations. Student fee revenue of $2.4 billion (11.9% growth over five years) declined $7 million year-over-year due to weaker international enrolment. Capital expenditures of $726 million include major projects at all three campuses. Salaries and benefits comprise $2.7 billion (60% of $4.5 billion expenses). Student financial support of $415 million (9.2% of expenses). Revenue growth of 1.6% insufficient to offset expense growth of 3.7% due to compensation increases, rising employee benefit costs, and geopolitical pressures.
documents.gradRelevance: Graduate students depend on the $415 million in student financial support (scholarships, bursaries, graduate awards), the $788 million in government grants supporting student enrolments, and the $2.7 billion in salaries and benefits that fund TA/RA positions. The 1.6% revenue growth versus 3.7% expense growth signals potential budget constraints affecting graduate funding. International student fee revenue decline signals reduced recruitment of international graduate students, and recalibrated international enrolment plans indicate tighter competition for international graduate student funding.

documents.title: External Auditors: Appointment for 2026-27
documents.summary: Document recommends re-appointment of Ernst & Young LLP (EY) as external auditors for fiscal year ending April 30, 2027, the second year of a five-year Master Services Agreement. EY was appointed following a formal Request for Proposals in fall 2023 and approved by Governing Council on June 27, 2024. New Audit Engagement Partner (Kathi Aspros) and Senior Manager assigned in fiscal 2025 to maintain independence. EY provides semi-annual reports on non-audit services to Audit Committee. University engages other firms for tax services and audits of government grant reports; separate auditors handle LEAP LP, UTSC Residence LP, and Spadina Sussex University residence audits.
documents.gradRelevance: No specific relevance to graduate students; auditor selection and financial oversight do not directly affect graduate student funding, fees, or services.

packages.title: Executive Committee - June 15, 2026
packages.summary: The Executive Committee held its June 15, 2026 meeting, addressing campus safety following October 7, 2025 concerns, approving the St. George Campus Plan 2040 for forwarding to Governing Council, endorsing the Build More Housing Initiative (37,165 sq m of student residence at 40-56 Harbord and 167-171 College Street, funded by Joint Venture Partnership), and approving the UTSC Field House project (7,194 gross sq m, funded by Athletic Centre Facility Levy). The Committee confirmed a new Master of Physician Assistant Studies program (effective September 1, 2027) and approved capital projects including Dentistry Clinic 2 Renewal (2,945 sq m) and Computer Science at Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus - West (3,486 sq m). The Provost reported research funding increased nearly $200 million over the previous year, Health & Wellness delivered 106,000 appointments in 2024-25, and second-year retention remained steady at 92.5%, though graduate employment declined reflecting market conditions.
packages.gradRelevance: Graduate students are directly affected by the new Master of Physician Assistant Studies program (effective September 1, 2027), expanding graduate credential options. The Build More Housing Initiative (37,165 sq m of on-campus residence) may provide housing options for graduate students, though details on allocation between undergraduate and graduate residents are not specified. The report notes the University of Toronto spends 73% more per student than Ontario averages on financial support and that Health & Wellness delivered over 106,000 mental health appointments in 2024-25, benefiting graduate students. Graduate employment rates declined, reflecting broader market conditions, but no specific graduate student stipend, TA/RA wage, or fee changes were discussed in this meeting.
keyItems: President reported India's Minister of Commerce and Industry visited Munk School on May 27, 2026, to strengthen commercial and bilateral trade relations, co-hosted by VP Joe Wong and Dean Susan Christoffersen

Federal government released AI strategy 'AI for All' on June 4, 2026, expanding CIFAR Artificial Intelligence Chairs program from 130 to 200 researchers; Prime Minister and Minister Evan Solomon (AI/Digital Innovation) visited Schwartz Reisman Innovation Campus and Vector Institute

Antisemitism initiatives underway: climate survey on Jewish and Israeli student experiences led by Professor Ron Levi at Lab for Global Study of Antisemitism, mandatory antisemitism training for Deans/Vice-Provosts/Principals, attestation forms on past discrimination complaints for incoming leadership

Campus Safety: 27 'hate crime/incidents' reported in St. George Campus Special Constable 2025 Annual Report; differences in reporting between St. George and UTM/UTSC attributable to definitional differences, to be reviewed before next annual reports; risk assessments ongoing across tri-campus
Policy on Human Rights Discrimination and Harassment in final consultation stages with UTFA, anticipated for approval in early 2026-27 governance cycles; Human Rights Office opening July 2026

Canvas/Quercus cyberattack: Quercus now operational; no evidence of other system compromise, no financial data/passwords disclosed, no core course content accessed; cyber security and vendor management enhancements underway

packages.title: University Affairs Board - October 23, 2025
packages.summary: At its Cycle 1 meeting the University Affairs Board worked through a heavily student-facing agenda: proposed revisions to the Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment plus the annual report under it, a presentation on the Hart House Building Renewal, the Multi-Year Accessibility Plan, and a motion to approve in principle the MacMillan Theatre Remediation (~1,200 gsm, Provostial funds).
packages.gradRelevance: The SVSH policy revisions set the protections, supports and complaint process covering grad students, TAs and RAs; the accessibility plan determines accommodations for disabled grad students; and Hart House is funded partly by student fees.
keyItems: Proposed revisions to the SVSH Policy (for information)
Annual report on activity under the SVSH Policy
MacMillan Theatre Remediation approved in principle (~1,200 gsm, Provostial funds)
Hart House Building Renewal presentation
Multi-Year Accessibility Plan (MYAP)

documents.title: Proposed Revisions: Policy on Sexual Violence and Sexual Harassment

documents.gradRelevance: Directly governs protections and reporting options for grad students, TAs and RAs.

documents.gradRelevance: Determines accommodations available to disabled grad students.
